title: On ergodicity of some Markov processes
overall_summary: The paper proves existence, uniqueness, ergodicity, and weak-star mean ergodicity criteria for Feller, stochastically continuous Markov processes on Polish spaces. Its central criterion combines the e-property, meaning uniform-in-time equicontinuity of P_t ψ at each point for every bounded Lipschitz observable ψ, with a lower bound on time-averaged transition probabilities of every neighborhood of a single point. The lower-bound condition is weaker than a Doeblin minorization because it need not be uniform in the initial state, and the method avoids assuming strong Feller or asymptotic strong Feller. For the original SPDE problem, the most relevant use is: if the C_0([0,1])-valued semigroup can be shown Feller/e-property and time-average irreducible toward some common state, then the paper gives at most one invariant probability measure; Theorem 3 gives a template for verifying the lower bound from a deterministic attractor, Lyapunov/tightness estimates, stochastic stability, and a common reachable point.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Basic setup and Definition 2.1 (e-property)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,ρ) be a Polish metric space. Let B_b(X), C_b(X), and Lip_b(X) denote bounded measurable, bounded continuous, and bounded Lipschitz real functions on X, respectively, and let Lip(f) be the best Lipschitz constant. The paper works with a transition semigroup (P_t)_{t≥0} of a Markov family Z = ((Z^x(t))_{t≥0}, x∈X), assumed throughout to be Feller, P_t(C_b(X))⊂C_b(X), and stochastically continuous, so P_t ψ(x)→ψ(x) as t↓0 for every ψ∈C_b(X). The semigroup has the e-property if for every ψ∈Lip_b(X), every x∈X, and every ε>0, there is δ>0 such that |P_t ψ(x)-P_t ψ(z)|<ε for all z∈B(x,δ) and all t≥0. A Markov family whose semigroup has the e-property is called an e-process.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition rather than a proved result. The paper repeatedly uses the e-property with bounded Lipschitz cutoff functions and finite sums of such cutoffs. The crucial feature is that the continuity modulus is uniform over all times t≥0, allowing comparisons of long-time averages started from nearby points without any decay or mixing estimate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1 (e-property plus pointwise lower bound gives uniqueness and convergence from tight points)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,ρ) be a Polish space and let (P_t)_{t≥0} be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup with the e-property. For μ∈M_1(X), define Q_T μ := T^{-1} ∫_0^T P_s^* μ ds, and write Q_T(x,·):=Q_T δ_x. Define T_set := {x∈X : the family (Q_T(x,·))_{T≥0} is tight}. Suppose there exists z∈X such that for every δ>0 and every x∈X, liminf_{T→∞} Q_T(x,B(z,δ))>0. Then (P_t)_{t≥0} admits a unique invariant probability measure μ*. Moreover, for every probability measure ν supported on T_set, Q_T ν converges weakly to μ* as T→∞.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes Theorem 3.1 of Lasota and Szarek [16] for existence of an invariant probability measure under these hypotheses. The main work is uniqueness and convergence. First, Proposition 1 shows that the support of any invariant probability measure is contained in T_set. The proof then establishes the key comparison: for any x_1,x_2∈T_set and any ψ∈Lip_b(X), lim_{T→∞}|∫ψ dQ_T(x_1)-∫ψ dQ_T(x_2)|=0. To prove this comparison, fix ε>0 and a decreasing sequence η_n↓0, and introduce a set Δ of weights α>0 for which, along times T_{α,n}≥n, the measures Q_{T_{α,n}}(x_i) can be approximated in total variation by measures μ^n_{α,i} that dominate a common weight α times probability measures ν^n_{α,i}, i=1,2, whose subsequent ergodic averages have ψ-integrals within ε in limsup. Lemma 3 proves Δ is nonempty and sup Δ=1. Given α∈Δ with α close to 1, decompose μ^n_{α,i}=α ν^n_{α,i}+residual. Lemma 2 says that inserting the fixed preliminary averaging time T_{α,n} before the long average Q_T has vanishing total-variation effect as T→∞. The residuals have total mass at most 1-α, so their contribution is small, while the ν^n_{α,i} contributions are close by the definition of Δ. Letting first T→∞, then n→∞, then α↑1 and ε↓0 gives the comparison. Finally, if μ is any invariant measure, Proposition 1 implies μ(T_set)=1; integrating the comparison against ν and μ gives ∫ψ dQ_Tν→∫ψ dμ for ψ∈Lip_b(X). Applying this with two invariant measures gives equality on bounded Lipschitz functions, hence uniqueness; applying it with the unique invariant μ* gives weak convergence Q_Tν⇒μ* for all ν supported in T_set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1 (separated compact masses from non-tightness)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (ν_n) be a sequence of probability measures on a Polish space X. If (ν_n) is not tight, then there exist ε>0, compact sets K_i⊂X, and an increasing sequence of positive integers n_i such that ν_{n_i}(K_i)≥ε for every i, and dist(K_i,K_j):=inf{ρ(x,y): x∈K_i, y∈K_j}≥ε whenever i≠j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this lemma from [16], pages 517-518, and does not reprove it. Its role is to convert failure of tightness into a sequence of well-separated compact sets carrying uniformly positive mass along a subsequence. This separation permits construction of a uniformly Lipschitz sum of bump functions supported near the K_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1 (support of invariant measures lies in the tight-time-average set)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (P_t)_{t≥0} be a Feller, stochastically continuous Markov semigroup on a Polish space X with the e-property. Suppose μ* is an invariant probability measure. Let T_set := {x∈X : (Q_T(x,·))_{T≥0} is tight}, where Q_T(x,·)=T^{-1}∫_0^T P_s^*δ_x ds. Then supp μ*⊂T_set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume for contradiction that x∈supp μ* but (Q_T(x,·)) is not tight. By Lemma 1, choose ε>0, times T_i↑∞, and compact sets K_i separated by distance at least ε, with Q_{T_i}(x,K_i)≥ε. The paper constructs inductively: a subsequence K_{m_n}; Lipschitz bump functions ~f_n satisfying 1_{K_{m_n}}≤~f_n≤1_{K_{m_n}^{ε/4}} and Lip(~f_n)≤4/ε; probability measures ν_n supported in B(x,1/n); and partial sums f_n=Σ_{i=1}^{n-1}~f_i. The measures ν_n are μ* conditioned on sufficiently small balls around x. Since ν_n≤c_n μ* and μ* is invariant, the family (P_t^*ν_n)_{t≥0} is tight; hence m_n can be chosen so far out that P_t^*ν_n(∪_{i=n}^∞ K_{m_i}^{ε/4})≤ε/4 uniformly in t. The e-property is applied to the already constructed finite sum f_n to choose the conditioning ball so that |P_t f_n(x)-P_t f_n(y)|<ε/4 for all y in supp ν_n and all t≥0. Because the K_{m_i}^{ε/4} remain separated, f:=Σ_i ~f_i is bounded and Lipschitz. Comparing Q_{T_{m_n}}(x) and Q_{T_{m_n}}ν_n on f gives a difference at least ε from the mass on K_{m_n}, minus ε/4 from the e-property control of f_n, minus ε/4 from the tail estimate, hence greater than ε/2. Therefore for each n there are t_n∈[0,T_{m_n}] and y_n∈suppν_n⊂B(x,1/n) such that P_{t_n}f(x)-P_{t_n}f(y_n)>ε/2. This contradicts equicontinuity of (P_t f)_{t≥0} at x. Thus x∈T_set.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2 (finite preliminary averaging is negligible in long time averages)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For t>0 define Q_t μ := t^{-1}∫_0^t P_s^* μ ds, and for t_1,...,t_k>0 write Q_{t_k,...,t_1} μ := Q_{t_k}⋯Q_{t_1} μ. Then for every k≥1 and every t_1,...,t_k>0, limsup_{T→∞} sup_{μ∈M_1(X)} ||Q_{T,t_k,...,t_1} μ - Q_T μ||_{TV}=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is written for k=1; the general case follows by induction. For fixed t_1, expand Q_T Q_{t_1} μ - Q_T μ as (T t_1)^{-1}∫_0^{t_1} dr [∫_0^T P_{s+r}^*μ ds - ∫_0^T P_s^*μ ds]. Shifting the first integral leaves only boundary intervals near 0 and T, so its total-variation norm is bounded by a constant times t_1/T, uniformly in μ. This tends to zero as T→∞. Iterating gives the result for finitely many preliminary Q_{t_i}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3 (mass-splitting weight can be boosted to one)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix ε>0, a decreasing sequence η_n↓0, points x_1,x_2∈T_set, and ψ∈Lip_b(X). Define Δ to be the set of α>0 for which there exist N, times T_{α,n}≥n, and probability measures μ^n_{α,i},ν^n_{α,i} for i=1,2 and n≥N satisfying ||Q_{T_{α,n}}(x_i)-μ^n_{α,i}||_{TV}<η_n, μ^n_{α,i}≥α ν^n_{α,i}, and limsup_{T→∞}|∫ψ dQ_Tν^n_{α,1}-∫ψ dQ_Tν^n_{α,2}|<ε. Under the hypotheses of Theorem 1, Δ is nonempty and sup Δ=1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Nonemptiness: by the e-property at the point z from Theorem 1, choose σ>0 so that |P_tψ(y)-P_tψ(z)|<ε/2 for y∈B(z,σ), all t≥0. The lower-bound hypothesis gives β>0 and T_0 such that Q_T(x_i,B(z,σ))≥β for all large T and i=1,2. Taking α=β, T_{α,n}=n+T_0, μ^n_{α,i}=Q_{T_{α,n}}(x_i), and ν^n_{α,i}=μ^n_{α,i} conditioned on B(z,σ), the ν-measures are supported where all P_tψ are ε-close to one another, yielding the required limsup bound. To prove sup Δ=1, suppose α_0:=sup Δ<1 and choose α_n∈Δ with α_n→α_0. Using x_i∈T_set and the total-variation approximation, the sequences μ_{n,i}:=μ^n_{α_n,i} and ν_{n,i}:=ν^n_{α_n,i} are tight; after subsequences they converge weakly. The residual measures ̄μ_{n,i}:=μ_{n,i}-α_nν_{n,i} converge to nonzero finite measures ̄μ_i because α_0<1. Choose y_i∈supp ̄μ_i. The lower-bound condition and the Feller property give a time T, γ>0, and radius r>0 such that Q_T(y,B(z,σ))≥γ/2 for all y∈B(y_i,r), i=1,2. Since the residuals put at least s_0/2 mass in B(y_i,r) for large n, applying Q_T to the residuals creates at least s_0γ/4 mass in B(z,σ). Conditioning this new mass gives measures ~μ_{n,i} supported in B(z,σ). Thus Q_T μ_{n,i} dominates α_n Q_Tν_{n,i} plus an additional common weight s_0γ/4 supported near z. To restore approximation to a long average of x_i, apply a further long average Q_{R_n}; Lemma 2 permits choosing R_n so that Q_{R_n,T,T_{n}}(x_i) and Q_{R_n}(x_i) differ by less than the remaining η_n margin. The new measures satisfy the definition of Δ with α'_0=α_0+s_0γ/8>α_0. The old component satisfies the required ψ-bound by the definition of Δ and Lemma 2; the new component satisfies it because both measures are supported in B(z,σ), where P_tψ is uniformly nearly constant. This contradiction proves sup Δ=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2 (uniform lower bound on bounded sets plus bounded-set tightness gives weak-star mean ergodicity and LLN)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (P_t)_{t≥0} satisfy the hypotheses of Theorem 1. Assume additionally that there exists z∈X such that for every bounded set A⊂X and every δ>0, inf_{x∈A} liminf_{T→∞} Q_T(x,B(z,δ))>0. Assume also that for every ε>0 and every x∈X there exists a bounded Borel set D⊂X such that liminf_{T→∞} Q_T(x,D)>1-ε. Then, in addition to the unique invariant probability measure μ* from Theorem 1, the semigroup is weak-star mean ergodic: Q_Tν⇒μ* for every ν∈M_1(X). Moreover, for every ψ∈Lip_b(X) and every initial distribution μ∈M_1(X), if (Z(t))_{t≥0} is the Markov process with law P_μ, then T^{-1}∫_0^T ψ(Z(t))dt converges in P_μ-probability to ∫ψ dμ*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first shows T_set=X, so Theorem 1 gives weak convergence of Q_Tν for every ν. The invariant measure μ* exists by Theorem 1. The uniform lower bound on bounded sets implies z∈supp μ*: choose a bounded Borel set B with μ*(B)>0 and use invariance μ*(B(z,δ))=∫Q_T(y,B(z,δ))μ*(dy), then Fatou and the uniform lower bound over B. Proposition 1 then gives z∈T_set. To show an arbitrary x lies in T_set, it suffices to find, for each ε>0, a closed set with finite ε-net carrying asymptotic Q_T(x)-mass greater than 1-ε. Since z∈T_set, choose such a finite-net set C_{ε/2} for Q_T(z); Lemma 4 uses the e-property to find a ball B(z,σ) such that every measure supported in B(z,σ) has long-time averaged mass almost all in the ε/2-neighborhood of C_{ε/2}, even after any fixed finite preliminary averaging. The proof then defines γ as the supremum total weight of components supported in B(z,σ) that can be dominated by finite averaged transitions starting from x. If γ≤1-ε/4, choose a near-optimal domination and call the remaining measure μ_t after an extra average Q_t. The bounded-set tightness assumption gives a bounded D with Q_t(x,D)>1-ε/8 for large t; Lemma 2 and γ≤1-ε/4 imply μ_t(D)>ε/16. The uniform lower bound from D to B(z,σ) then forces a further Q_T μ_t component of weight at least order ε inside B(z,σ), contradicting near-optimality of γ. Hence γ>1-ε/4, and Lemma 4 implies the desired finite-net tightness for Q_T(x). For the weak law of large numbers, expectation convergence follows from weak-star mean ergodicity. For the second moment, the Markov property gives E_μ[(T^{-1}∫_0^Tψ(Z(t))dt)^2] = 2T^{-2}∫_0^T (T-s) ∫ P_s(ψ Ψ_{T-s}) dμ ds, where Ψ_t(x)=∫ψ dQ_t(x)=t^{-1}∫_0^tP_rψ(x)dr. Lemma 5 gives uniform convergence of Ψ_t to d*:=∫ψ dμ* on compact sets. Choosing a compact K with Q_tμ(K^c) small uniformly in t, one replaces Ψ_{T-s} by d* on K and controls K^c by boundedness of ψ. The remaining term tends d*^2, proving convergence in probability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4 (e-property propagates tightness from z to nearby starting laws)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the proof of Theorem 2, fix ε>0. Suppose z∈T_set and choose a closed set C_{ε/2} with a finite ε/2-net such that liminf_{T→∞}Q_T(z,C_{ε/2})>1-ε/2. Let ~C be the ε/2-neighborhood of C_{ε/2}. Then there exists σ>0 such that inf_{ν∈M_1(B(z,σ))} liminf_{T→∞} Q_Tν(~C)>1-3ε/4. Moreover, for any k≥1 and any t_1,...,t_k≥0, there is T_* such that inf_{ν∈M_1(B(z,σ))} Q_{T,t_1,...,t_k}ν(~C)>1-3ε/4 for every T≥T_*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose a Lipschitz cutoff φ with 1_{C_{ε/2}}≤φ≤1_{~C}. By the e-property at z, choose σ>0 so that |P_tφ(y)-P_tφ(z)|<ε/4 for all y∈B(z,σ), t≥0. Therefore Q_T(y,~C)≥∫φ dQ_T(y)≥∫φ dQ_T(z)-ε/4≥Q_T(z,C_{ε/2})-ε/4, and taking liminf gives the first assertion uniformly over y∈B(z,σ), hence over probability measures supported there. The second assertion follows by applying Lemma 2, which says fixed preliminary averaging Q_{t_1},...,Q_{t_k} changes the long average by vanishing total variation as T→∞.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5 (uniform convergence of ergodic averages on compact sets)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Under the hypotheses and conclusion of Theorem 2, let ψ∈Lip_b(X), let μ* be the unique invariant measure, and define Ψ_t(x):=∫ψ(y)Q_t(x,dy)=t^{-1}∫_0^tP_sψ(x)ds. For every ε>0 and every compact K⊂X, there exists t_0>0 such that sup_{x∈K}|Ψ_t(x)-∫ψ dμ*|<ε for all t≥t_0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Weak-star mean ergodicity gives pointwise convergence Ψ_t(x)→∫ψ dμ* for every x∈X. The e-property implies equicontinuity of the family (Ψ_t)_{t≥0}: for a fixed x and η>0, choose a neighborhood on which |P_sψ(x)-P_sψ(y)|<η for all s≥0, then average in s. A compact covering argument gives equicontinuity on compact K. Pointwise convergence plus equicontinuity on a compact set yields uniform convergence, which the paper phrases via the Arzela-Ascoli theorem.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3 (verification criterion for stochastic evolution equations in Hilbert spaces)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a real separable Hilbert space. Consider the stochastic evolution equation dZ(t)=(AZ(t)+F(Z(t)))dt+R dW(t), where A generates a C_0-semigroup S(t) on X, F maps D(F)⊂X into X, R is a bounded linear operator from another Hilbert space H to X, and W is a cylindrical Wiener process on H. Assume that for every x∈X there is a unique mild solution Z^x, that these solutions form a stochastically continuous Feller Markov family, and that the deterministic equation dY(t)/dt=AY(t)+F(Y(t)), Y(0)=x, defines a continuous semi-dynamical system. Suppose: (i) the deterministic system has a compact global attractor K, meaning K is invariant and attracts every bounded set; (ii) there is a Lyapunov function Φ:X→[0,∞), measurable, bounded on bounded sets, and Φ(x)→∞ as ||x||_X→∞, such that sup_{t≥0}EΦ(Z^x(t))<∞ for every x∈X; (iii) the stochastic family is stochastically stable, meaning for all ε,R,t>0, inf_{x∈B(0,R)}P(||Z^x(t)-Y^x(t)||_X<ε)>0, and the common reachability condition ∩_{x∈K} ∪_{t≥0} Γ_t(x) is nonempty, where Γ_t(x)=supp(P_t^*δ_x); (iv) the transition semigroup has the e-property. Then the Markov family admits a unique invariant probability measure μ*, is weak-star mean ergodic, and for every bounded Lipschitz ψ, T^{-1}∫_0^Tψ(Z^x(t))dt converges in probability to ∫ψ dμ*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The theorem is proved by verifying the hypotheses of Theorem 2. Condition (2.9) of Theorem 2 follows from the Lyapunov bound and Chebyshev's inequality, since sublevel sets of Φ are bounded. The e-property is assumed. It remains to prove the uniform lower bound on bounded sets. Step I: for each y∈K, Lyapunov and Chebyshev give a bounded set B_y^0 with liminf_T Q_T(y,B_y^0)>3/4. Choose an open bounded B_y⊃B_y^0 and a continuous cutoff. The e-property gives a radius r_y such that starting from any point in B(y,r_y), the liminf mass in B_y is >1/2. A finite cover of compact K yields a bounded B and r_* with liminf_T Q_T(x,B)>1/2 for all x∈K+r_*B(0,1). Step II: for any bounded D, the global attractor gives L such that Y^x(L) is within r_*/2 of K for all x∈D; stochastic stability gives a uniform positive probability p that Z^x(L) is within r_*/2 of Y^x(L). Combining this with Step I through the Markov property and Fatou gives liminf_T Q_T(x,B)>γ>0 uniformly for x∈D. Step III: for any bounded D and radius r>0, use Step II to first reach the bounded set B in time-average, then stochastic stability from B to K+rB(0,1), obtaining a positive uniform lower bound on liminf_T Q_T(x,K+rB(0,1)). Step IV: choose z in the common reachable set. For every x∈K choose t_x with z∈supp P_{t_x}^*δ_x. By the Feller property, each such lower bound persists on a neighborhood of x. A finite cover of K yields a finite set of times S and a radius ~r such that inf_{y∈K+~rB(0,1)} max_{s∈S}P_s1_{B(z,δ)}(y)>0. Step V: combine Step III with r=~r and Step IV, then average over shifted times: Σ_{s∈S}T^{-1}∫_0^T P_{q+s}1_{B(z,δ)}(x)dq is bounded below by a positive constant uniformly over x in the bounded D. Since this sum equals #S times liminf_T Q_T(x,B(z,δ)) up to negligible endpoints, the desired uniform lower bound (2.8) follows. Theorem 2 then yields uniqueness, weak-star mean ergodicity, and the law of large numbers.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 5.1.4, estimate (5.17) and Proposition 2 (Malliavin-control proof of the e-property in the paper's SPDE application)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the passive-tracer Sobolev setting of Sections 5-6, under assumptions (2.16) and (2.17), the Markov family solves dZ(t)=[AZ(t)+B(Z(t),Z(t))]dt+Q^{1/2}dW(t) on the Hilbert space X=X^m, where A is diagonal with Fourier symbol -γ(k), Q is diagonal with symbol γ(k)E(k), and B is the bilinear form B(ψ,φ)(ξ)=(Σ_j ψ_j(0) ∂φ_i/∂ξ_j(ξ))_{i=1}^d. The key estimate is: for every ψ∈C_b^1(X) and R>0, there is C such that sup_{t≥0} sup_{||x||_X≤R} ||D P_tψ(x)||_X ≤ C ||ψ||_{C_b^1(X)}. This estimate implies the e-property because every bounded Lipschitz ψ can be approximated pointwise by smooth cylindrical ψ_n∈C_b^1(X) with uniformly bounded sup norms and Lipschitz constants. Proposition 2 supplies the estimate: for every R>0 and every x,v∈X with ||x||_X≤R and ||v||_X≤1, there exists an adapted X-valued process g_t=g_t(v,x) such that sup_{||x||≤R,||v||≤1}∫_0^∞E||Q^{1/2}g_s||_X^2 ds<∞ and sup_{||x||≤R,||v||≤1}sup_{t≥0}E||D_xZ^x(t)[v]-D_gZ^x(t)||_X<∞, where D_x denotes derivative with respect to the initial condition and D_g denotes the Malliavin derivative in direction g.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For smooth ψ, D P_tψ(x)[v]=E[Dψ(Z^x(t))[D_xZ^x(t)[v]]]. The initial derivative U(t)=D_xZ^x(t)[v] satisfies the linearized equation dU/dt=AU+B(Z^x,U)+B(U,Z^x), U(0)=v. The Malliavin derivative D_gZ^x(t) satisfies the same linearized equation with an added forcing Q^{1/2}g(t) and zero initial condition. If g is chosen as in Proposition 2 and ρ_t:=D_xZ^x(t)[v]-D_gZ^x(t), then the chain rule and Malliavin integration by parts give E[Dψ(Z^x(t))[D_gZ^x(t)]]=E[ψ(Z^x(t))∫_0^t⟨g(s),Q^{1/2}dW(s)⟩_X]. Cauchy-Schwarz and Itô isometry control this term by ||ψ||_∞(E∫||Q^{1/2}g||^2)^{1/2}, while the ρ_t term is bounded by ||Dψ||_∞E||ρ_t||. Proposition 2 therefore yields the uniform gradient estimate. To prove Proposition 2, the paper splits Fourier modes into high and low modes using projections Π_{≥N} and Π_{<N}. It defines ζ_N by a controlled linearized equation retaining the high-mode dynamics and adding a low-mode damping term -1/2 Π_{<N}ζ_N ||Π_{<N}ζ_N||^{-1}. It then defines f(t) to be exactly the low-mode terms and damping needed so that ζ_N solves the difference equation for D_xZ^x-D_gZ^x, and sets g=Q^{-1/2}f, which is legitimate on the finite-dimensional low-mode space where Q is invertible. Lemma 8 shows the low modes of ζ_N vanish after time 2. Lemma 9 gives uniform fourth-moment bounds and integrability of ζ_N, using dissipativity of A, moment estimates for Z^x, and high-mode smallness of the Ornstein-Uhlenbeck stochastic convolution. These bounds imply both the finite energy of Q^{1/2}g and the uniform bound on ρ_t=ζ_N.

proof_techniques.name: E-property as a substitute for strong Feller
proof_techniques.description: The paper does not require P_t to map bounded measurable functions to continuous functions. Instead, it assumes that for each bounded Lipschitz observable ψ, the family (P_tψ)_{t≥0} is equicontinuous at every point, uniformly in time. This is weaker than strong Feller in many infinite-dimensional settings but strong enough to control long-time averaged observables started from nearby initial points.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the C_0([0,1])-valued SPDE, one possible route to uniqueness is to prove the e-property for P_t directly, or for the Gaussian-drift approximations and then pass to the limit. This can bypass proving strong Feller for the singular δ_0 drift.

proof_techniques.name: Time-averaged lower bound around one point
proof_techniques.description: The core irreducibility condition is not a one-step minorization and not uniform in the initial state: for a single z∈X, every initial x and every δ>0 must satisfy liminf_T Q_T(x,B(z,δ))>0. This condition says every trajectory has positive asymptotic time-average probability of entering every neighborhood of z. Theorems 1 and 2 show that, combined with the e-property and tightness, this is enough for uniqueness and mean ergodicity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the SPDE, a strategic target is to identify a common point such as 0 or a deterministic equilibrium and prove that the time-averaged law of u(t) gives positive mass to every C_0-neighborhood of that point from every initial condition.

proof_techniques.name: Mass-splitting and alpha-boosting
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1 introduces a set Δ of weights α for which two long-time averaged laws can each be decomposed into a common weight α of components whose future averages are close, plus residuals. The lower-bound hypothesis and e-property give some α>0. If sup Δ<1, tightness gives weak limits of residuals, the lower bound sends a positive part of each residual back near the common point z, and equicontinuity near z makes those new parts compatible; this increases α and contradicts maximality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a way to prove uniqueness without constructing an explicit coupling of two SPDE solutions. It is enough to repeatedly extract common small components in neighborhoods where P_tψ is uniformly continuous.

proof_techniques.name: Support-of-invariant-measures tightness argument
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 1 shows that for an e-process, any invariant measure can charge only initial states whose ergodic averages are tight. The contradiction uses separated compact sets carrying escaping mass, Lipschitz bump functions supported on those sets, and invariant-measure conditioning near a support point to violate the e-property.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If invariant measures for the SPDE exist but global tightness from all initial states is hard, this proposition indicates that uniqueness may only need comparison on the tight set where invariant measures live.

proof_techniques.name: Tightness via compact finite-net sets and local propagation from z
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 2, the authors prove T_set=X by using tightness of z, e-property propagation to a small ball around z, and a supremum argument over dominated components supported in that ball. Finite ε-net sets replace compactness in nonlocally compact Polish spaces, and Lemma 2 handles fixed preliminary time averages.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a C_0-valued heat SPDE, compactness can often be obtained from Hölder or Sobolev regularity estimates via Arzela-Ascoli. One can try to combine such estimates with a lower bound into a small ball around the common point.

proof_techniques.name: Deterministic attractor plus stochastic stability
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3 verifies the abstract lower-bound condition by comparing the stochastic dynamics to the deterministic semi-flow. If the deterministic system has a compact global attractor, stochastic stability gives positive probability to follow the deterministic flow into a neighborhood of the attractor, and a common reachable point from the attractor yields the lower bound for all bounded initial sets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A solver could examine whether the driftless or deterministic part of the SPDE has a compact attracting set and whether small-noise support or support-theorem arguments imply stochastic stability toward it.

proof_techniques.name: Lyapunov functions for bounded-set tightness
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Lyapunov functions Φ that are bounded on bounded sets and diverge at infinity to obtain bounded-set recurrence by Chebyshev's inequality. Remark 2 also notes a common SPDE route: if there is a compactly embedded space V into X and a function Φ(r)→∞ with sup_{t≥T_0}EΦ(||Z^x(t)||_V)<∞, then time-averaged laws are tight in X.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the stochastic heat equation on [0,1], heat-kernel smoothing and moment estimates may give compactness in C_0 through Hölder norms or fractional Sobolev norms, supplying the tightness input needed by Theorem 1 or 2.

proof_techniques.name: Malliavin-control gradient bounds for e-property
proof_techniques.description: In the SPDE application, the paper proves the e-property by first proving a uniform-in-time gradient estimate for P_tψ on bounded sets for ψ∈C_b^1. It decomposes the derivative with respect to initial data into a Malliavin derivative in a chosen adapted direction plus a controlled remainder; integration by parts estimates the Malliavin part using the energy of the control, while dissipativity estimates the remainder.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If direct equicontinuity of P_t is difficult for the singular drift, one could try to prove uniform gradient/equicontinuity estimates for the smooth approximating SPDEs with drift G_{1/n}, then use the assumed weak convergence u^n(t)⇒u(t) to transfer the e-property or enough equicontinuity.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform convergence of averaged observables on compact sets
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 5 combines pointwise weak-star mean ergodicity with e-property equicontinuity to upgrade convergence of Ψ_t(x)=t^{-1}∫_0^tP_sψ(x)ds to uniform convergence on compact sets. This is then inserted into a second-moment calculation for additive functionals to prove a weak law of large numbers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original question is only uniqueness of invariant measures, this technique could be useful if a proof strategy also seeks ergodic averages or needs uniform control of time-averaged observables on compact subsets of C_0([0,1]).

other_useful_info: The paper emphasizes that in nonlocally compact Polish spaces a lower bound over a thin set is not by itself enough for existence of an invariant measure; tightness or compactness input is essential, and a counterexample is cited from Szarek [26]. Remark 2 gives a practical SPDE tightness criterion: if Z^x(t) takes values in a Hilbert/Banach state space X and there is a compactly embedded space V into X plus a locally bounded Φ with Φ(R)→∞ and sup_{t≥T_0}EΦ(||Z^x(t)||_V)<∞, then x belongs to the tight set T_set. Remark 3 states that weak-star mean ergodicity of a stochastically continuous Feller semigroup implies uniqueness and ergodicity of the invariant measure. Remark 5 notes that the common reachability condition in Theorem 3 is trivial when the deterministic attractor K is a singleton, and also holds in nondegenerate-noise situations where the attractor is contained in the support of the transition law from the attractor. The introduction positions the e-property criterion as an alternative to strong Feller or asymptotic strong Feller methods, with related references Da Prato-Zabczyk [3], Hairer-Mattingly [11,12], and Lasota-Szarek [16].
why_relevant: This paper gives Polish-space criteria for existence and uniqueness of invariant measures using the e-property and lower bounds for transition probabilities. These criteria are potentially applicable to C0([0,1])-valued SPDEs where strong Feller may be difficult to establish.
